text: The surgeon mobilizes the splenic angle and then ligates the inferior mesenteric vein and artery using infrared technology.

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision by using a diatomy hook and swab to assess the posterior tissue plane, ensuring a precise dissection. The procedure progresses anteriorly and laterally to achieve a safe margin beneath the tumor.
The surgeon staples the rectal tube below the tumor after completely freeing the mesorectum, potentially requiring multiple staple lines.
The rectal stem is visible at the bottom of the pelvis, prepared for a new anastomosis between the colon and rectum. The specimen is extracted through a suprapubic incision.

The surgeon divides the mesenteric vessels at the predetermined level and assesses the viability of the remaining bowel using indocyanine green. The video shows a sequence of views: a normal view, a black and white view, and an augmented reality view where indocyanine green appears in green and areas lacking vascularization appear in black or normal light.

The surgeon observes a clear cut-off indicating the viability of the bowel for anastomosis. A low situate anastomosis is performed, followed by an assessment of the anastomosis and microvascularization. The screen displays three views: normal, black and white, and augmented reality with indocyanine green highlighting vascularization.

Following the intravenous injection of indocyanine green, the fluorescent signal appears first on the colonic side, highlighting the staple line in normal light. The rectal stem, which appears pale, especially post-irradiation, also shows the microvascularization pattern with visible microvessels along its surface.
The surgeon uses a spatial rectoscope to assess the internal view of the rectal area, ensuring satisfactory surgical outcomes and microvascularization patterns before concluding the procedure.
The final view shows the anastomosis in enhanced imaging after performing a low anterior resection, with a protective ileostomy and pelvic drainage visible.
Further study are needed to define if we still need in case a very good signal of a drain or an idiostomy. Thank you for your attention.
